video_title: MacroVoices #541 Dr. Anas Alhajji: Bab el-Mandeb: The Next Oil Chokepoint Nobody's Watching
gist: Dr. Anas Alhajji argues the Strait of Hormuz was deliberately closed by the US (via an EU insurance-law mechanism, not military action) to demonstrate energy/AI dominance over China, that rogue IRGC factions rather than Iran's negotiators are now blocking reopening, and that Bab el-Mandeb, refining capacity, and SPR policy are the next fronts investors should watch, with LNG and coal as the clearest long-term winners.

insights.claim: A Houthi attack on the Saudi airport in Abha effectively ended the informal Saudi-Houthi agreement that had kept Bab el-Mandeb calm, making it the next flashpoint to watch after Hormuz.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Watch for renewed Houthi-Saudi hostilities as a leading indicator of a second insurance-driven chokepoint shock.

insights.claim: Roughly six million barrels per day of oil, mostly Russian and Saudi crude, transits Bab el-Mandeb, the entrance to the Red Sea.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Sizes the volume at risk if the strait is disrupted, a scale large enough to move global benchmarks.
insights.quote: about six million barrels of oil basically passes through Babel Mandib mostly Russian and Saudi oil

insights.claim: The real risk to Bab el-Mandeb isn't the Houthis physically blockading it, but a single tanker attack causing European (mostly UK) insurers to cancel war-risk coverage, replicating the mechanism that shut Hormuz.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Investors should track marine war-risk insurance pricing/cancellations as the real transmission mechanism for chokepoint shocks, not just headline attack counts.
insights.quote: the fear is not the Houthis blocking Babel Mandi. The fear is that few guys basically launching an attack on couple of oil tankers and as a result the insurance companies...will literally cancel the insurance

insights.claim: If Bab el-Mandeb closes, Saudi Arabia would lose more than four million barrels a day of crude flow, since that is the same oil already being diverted from the Gulf through east-west pipelines because of the Hormuz crisis.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: A Bab el-Mandeb closure would compound rather than diversify the existing Hormuz supply risk, since it's the same barrels' only remaining export route.

insights.claim: Russia would be a relative winner if Bab el-Mandeb insurance seized up, because its exports to India and China already move on sanctioned tankers insured outside the Western market.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: A shadow-fleet/insurance bifurcation means Western sanctions-linked disruptions can inadvertently strengthen Russia's competitive position in Asian crude markets.

insights.claim: Alhajji expects any Bab el-Mandeb disruption to be short-lived given a severe Arab-coalition and US response, but insurance costs will stay structurally higher even after the strait reopens, just as happened with Hormuz.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Expect a persistent war-risk insurance premium embedded in shipping costs even after any acute event resolves, a structural tailwind for freight/insurance-linked plays and a floor under landed oil costs.

insights.claim: Alhajji publicly predicted roughly a year before the Iran conflict began that the Strait of Hormuz would be closed, but by the United States rather than Iran, as a message to China about US energy and AI dominance.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: A track record of correctly calling the closure mechanism a year in advance lends weight to Alhajji's broader US-China dominance thesis for positioning going forward.

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz was effectively closed without any direct military action: an EU solvency law forced European war-risk insurers to cancel coverage across the whole Indian Ocean within seven days after a US Navy strike near Sirri Island killed about 85 Iranian sailors.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Reveals an insurance-regulation transmission channel for chokepoint shocks that can shut shipping lanes faster than military blockades, and that could recur at any strait if a similar trigger event occurs.
insights.quote: I said that Iran cannot close the straight but the United States will close the straight and I strongly believe that is exactly what happened

insights.claim: Iran did not actually close Hormuz; it began claiming credit for the closure only after Western insurers had already halted coverage, similar to a gangster who shows up to guard a mall that's already locked for the night.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Separates the narrative (Iran 'closing' the strait) from the actual mechanism (insurance cancellation), which matters for correctly forecasting when and how the strait could reopen or close again.
insights.quote: just like a gangster goes to the mall after it closed after 10 p.m. with his gun and he stood in front of the door

insights.claim: China anticipated the Hormuz closure over a year in advance by monitoring Arabic-language energy commentary (including Alhajji's own Arabic-language writing) and began pre-building oil, natural gas, and coal inventories well before the conflict started.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: China's information-gathering and stockpiling advantage suggests it will continue to front-run Western-facing geopolitical energy shocks, a structural edge worth tracking in Chinese import/inventory data.

insights.claim: Alhajji's central thesis is that the Hormuz closure was a 'deep state' strategy, consistent with the Trump administration's November 2025 National Security Strategy, to project US dominance in energy and AI over China rather than primarily a response to Iran's nuclear program.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: If correct, the conflict's resolution will be driven by US-China strategic signaling dynamics, not purely by Iran-nuclear negotiations, changing how investors should read de-escalation headlines.

insights.claim: A helium/LNG plant in Qatar, the source of roughly 75% of the helium used by South Korea and Taiwan for semiconductor manufacturing, was destroyed early in the war, cutting off a critical chip-industry input for Asia.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: A helium supply shock adds a second, less-watched constraint on Asian chip production alongside energy costs, reinforcing the reshoring case for US/Arizona semiconductor capacity.

insights.claim: Regional oil benchmarks diverged sharply from headline Brent/WTI: Dubai/Oman medium sour crude spiked above $170 with some spot cargoes near $200, meaning the real price shock to Asian buyers was much larger than Brent and WTI suggested.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Analysts anchored only to Brent/WTI missed the actual magnitude of demand destruction in Asia; regional sour-crude benchmarks are the more accurate gauge of stress during chokepoint events.

themes.theme: IRGC Rogue Factions as the Wildcard

insights.claim: The Iranian regime is not a unified actor; IRGC hardliners operate outside the elected government, have grown wealthy from sanctions-era smuggling, and have a direct financial incentive to prevent normalization.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Negotiation headlines with Tehran's official negotiators may not translate into actual de-escalation as long as IRGC hardliners retain independent operational control and financial motive to keep tensions elevated.

insights.claim: The extremist IRGC faction driving current attacks functions like an international cartel with reach into Yemen (via Houthi factions), Lebanon, and Iraq, and even a presence in Mexico, Venezuela, and Argentina.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: The network's geographic reach means chokepoint risk isn't confined to Hormuz or Bab el-Mandeb; the same actors have latent capacity to project disruption more broadly.

insights.claim: US and Iranian negotiators may both be negotiating in good faith, but a resolution is blocked because rogue IRGC elements, who don't represent the elected government, actually control the leverage over the strait.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: A resolution requires the US to militarily degrade IRGC extremist elements, not just reach a diplomatic deal with Tehran's official team, implying a longer and more violent path to reopening than headline diplomacy suggests.

insights.claim: Fiery public statements from both the Trump administration and Iranian officials are largely theater for domestic audiences and should not be read as signals that a full-scale war is resuming.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Traders should discount rhetoric-driven price spikes on official statements unless corroborated by actual attacks, since much of the messaging is aimed at domestic politics rather than signaling escalation intent.

insights.claim: A full collapse of the Iranian regime, not just a change of government, would be the worst-case outcome for the region and Western Europe, since it would trigger a refugee exodus toward Europe and stoke Kurdish and Baloch separatism in Turkey and Pakistan.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Because no regional or Western power wants regime collapse, expect US policy to continue targeting IRGC extremist elements specifically rather than pursuing broader regime change, limiting tail-risk of state failure.

insights.claim: An attack on an LNG carrier was the actual 'red line' that triggered a rapid US retaliation, more so than other attacks, indicating the US is prioritizing protection of LNG shipping specifically.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: LNG carrier safety is functioning as a tripwire for US military response, making LNG shipping routes a useful leading indicator for de-escalation or re-escalation.

insights.claim: China cut oil imports by roughly six million barrels a day when Dubai/Oman crude spiked above $170, a deliberate 'buy low, sell high' response consistent with its demand behavior over the past decade.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: China's import elasticity at high prices is a repeatable, trackable signal; large import cuts should be read as price discipline, not necessarily distress.

insights.claim: China's six-million-barrel import cut was not mainly drawn from visible onshore inventories (only about 50 million barrels were used); it broke down into roughly 0.8-1.0 million b/d of halted inventory-building, about 1.5 million b/d absorbed into non-satellite-visible floating storage, higher domestic production, and a ban on refined-product exports.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Popular narratives that tracked only onshore/satellite-visible Chinese inventories overstated the real drawdown and understated China's resilience; floating storage should be added to any China demand model.

insights.claim: China's official Q2 2026 GDP growth print was the lowest since the 1990s, partly reflecting genuine demand destruction from high oil prices layered on top of the ongoing shift to electric vehicles.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Confirms that even China's demand-management playbook has a real economic growth cost, which caps how long the strategy can be sustained.

insights.claim: China's import-cutting strategy is only sustainable in the short-to-medium term; if the Hormuz situation dragged on for another year, Alhajji believes China would become the biggest economic loser of the crisis.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: A prolonged chokepoint crisis would eventually force China to either resume higher-priced imports or accept deeper economic damage, a scenario worth stress-testing in China-linked positions.

insights.claim: China has been preparing since around 2019 for a potential closure of the Malacca Strait amid expectations of war or sanctions, which explains its heavy build-out of solar, wind, hydro, and nuclear power as an energy-security hedge rather than primarily a climate policy.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Reframes China's renewable buildout as strategic energy-security insurance rather than climate virtue, implying continued state-backed investment in domestic power generation regardless of climate-policy shifts elsewhere.
insights.quote: they were convinced that Malika strait will be closed. So China basically is preparing for the Malika strait to be closed

themes.theme: Refining Capacity Is the Real Bottleneck

insights.claim: US refineries are running near 96-97% of capacity on average (some individual refineries above 100%), so even a surge in gasoline and diesel demand cannot pull through significantly more crude, undermining the bullish 'tank-bottom' narrative for crude prices.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Crude-price bulls betting on a demand-driven price spike from low product inventories are likely mispricing the constraint; the binding limit is refining throughput, not crude availability, capping WTI/Brent upside even amid a product squeeze.
insights.quote: when refineries are running at almost full capacity, even if there is massive demand for gasoline and diesel, if you cannot process the crude, then you are not going to demand the crude

insights.claim: The Trump administration's 'energy dominance' policy focused on upstream production and neglected downstream refining capacity, which Alhajji calls the biggest structural criticism of the policy.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Any durable US energy-dominance strategy requires new refinery capacity or expansions; until that happens, US refined-product exports and domestic prices remain structurally constrained.

insights.claim: The global diesel crisis was driven by the shutdown of exports from three major Gulf refineries (in Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE), compounded by panic-driven export restrictions in China, India, and South Korea and by tankers being resold and re-routed multiple times mid-voyage.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Diesel scarcity was amplified by policy panic and hoarding, not just physical supply loss, meaning the crisis could recur even without a fresh chokepoint event if similar export-restriction panic sets in.

insights.claim: Without US SPR releases of medium sour crude, domestic diesel prices in the United States could have reached roughly $12 a gallon.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the real economic value of SPR releases beyond headline barrel counts, arguing for their strategic (not just fiscal) justification.
insights.quote: without the US strategic petroleum reserve releases, diesel prices in the United States probably would be in in the range of $12 a gallon

insights.claim: US diesel exports reached record highs because SPR releases of medium sour crude let domestic refiners cut their own medium-sour import needs, freeing more medium sour crude for the rest of the world while the US exported more refined diesel.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: US refiners and diesel exporters are direct beneficiaries of SPR release policy, a dynamic likely to continue as long as releases persist.

themes.theme: SPR Mechanics: Loans, Limits, and Leverage

insights.claim: The widely cited '250 million barrel floor' for the US Strategic Petroleum Reserve is a legal limit set by Congress, not a technical one, and the law allows the president to authorize releases below that floor by declaring an emergency.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: The SPR has more usable capacity than headlines suggest, meaning further large releases remain legally available if the administration wants to suppress prices further, a bearish tail risk for crude bulls.

insights.claim: The US could technically draw the SPR down by roughly another 100 million barrels beyond its current level without hitting real geological constraints, since the underground salt caverns are filled with brine, not air, when oil is withdrawn.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Technical cavern-integrity concerns cited by bulls as a hard floor on SPR drawdowns are overstated, removing one of the bullish arguments for a near-term SPR-driven price floor.

insights.claim: As a net oil exporter, the United States is exempt from the International Energy Agency's rule requiring members to hold 90 days of net oil imports in strategic reserves, giving it more legal room to draw the SPR down than import-dependent IEA peers.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Removes another commonly cited institutional constraint on further SPR drawdowns, reinforcing that policy choice, not rules, will determine future release pace.

insights.claim: SPR releases (about 99 million barrels released so far, with roughly 73 million barrels of authorized releases remaining) are structured as loans, not sales: companies must return the borrowed oil plus an in-kind interest premium, and because they borrowed near $120/barrel and are repaying when oil trades near $60-80, oil companies and traders are profiting substantially at taxpayer expense.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Companies holding SPR loan obligations have an embedded profit motive to refill at low prices, which argues against the bullish thesis that SPR refilling itself will drive prices materially higher.
insights.quote: they borrowed the oil at 120 and later on when they return it, they will return it when oil is 60 or 70 or 80

insights.claim: Because SPR releases peaked at up to 1.9 million barrels a day while technical injection (refill) capacity is limited to roughly 400,000 barrels a day, the SPR refill process will only create a soft price floor rather than driving crude prices meaningfully higher.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Investors betting on an SPR-refill-driven crude rally should temper expectations; the asymmetry between release and injection rates limits the bullish impact to a price floor, not a rally.

insights.claim: Japan's SPR drawdown was driven primarily by yen weakness, which pushed oil priced in yen to an all-time high in April, rather than by physical shortage; Japan will not refill its reserves until the yen strengthens, unlike India, which rebuilt its inventories to their highest level since COVID.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Japan's SPR refill timeline is a currency call, not an oil-market call; JPY strength (or weakness) is now a direct input into future Japanese crude demand.

themes.theme: Post-Hormuz World: OPEC+ and the Permanent Risk Premium

insights.claim: Alhajji proposes OPEC+ reform bylaws should shift from measuring member production to measuring exports, should track all liquids rather than just crude, and should partner with consuming nations to jointly build strategic reserves in locations free of maritime choke points.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: An OPEC+ pivot toward export-based rules and jointly-funded strategic reserves would be a structural shift in how the cartel manages supply signaling, worth monitoring in future OPEC+ communiques.

insights.claim: Alhajji proposes OPEC+ fund strategic petroleum reserves in importing countries like India, which lack the capital to build them alone, in exchange for those countries later exporting refined oil during a Hormuz-type closure, effectively converting large importers into quasi-OPEC+ exporters.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Watch for OPEC+ capital deployment into third-country storage infrastructure as a new form of producer-consumer strategic alliance that could reshape crude flows during future chokepoint crises.

insights.claim: New cross-border oil pipeline and canal megaprojects (such as routes through Syria or Israel) no longer make security sense given cheap drone-strike capability demonstrated in the Ukraine war; only projects with independent economic logic, like the UAE's Fujairah pipeline, are worth building.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Investors should be skeptical of chokepoint-bypass pipeline proposals pitched primarily on security grounds; only projects with standalone economic returns (like UAE's Fujairah route) are likely to actually get built.

insights.claim: The UAE's departure from OPEC does not threaten the cartel's cohesion, because no OPEC+ member wants to trigger a price war, so OPEC+ will remain functionally intact.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Headlines about OPEC+ fragmentation from the UAE exit are likely overstated as a bearish catalyst for cartel discipline.

insights.claim: Even after a formal resolution to the Hormuz crisis, an elevated 'ghost of Hormuz' risk premium will persist indefinitely because any militia, disgruntled group, or even a trader with a large social-media following can now trigger price spikes with unverified attack claims.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Structurally higher volatility and headline-driven whipsaws in oil prices should be expected as a permanent feature of the market, favoring options/volatility strategies over simple directional crude bets.
insights.quote: we created a monster that no one can slain period

themes.theme: Investment Positioning: LNG, Coal, and the Market Wrap

insights.claim: Alhajji's top investment recommendation is US LNG names, calling natural gas and especially US LNG infrastructure the biggest structural winner from the combination of Hormuz-driven energy-security concerns and rising AI/data-center power demand.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Positions US LNG producers/exporters as a direct long-term beneficiary of both geopolitical risk premia and structural AI power demand growth.
insights.quote: I am very big on LNG names.

insights.claim: Coal is positioned as a secondary winner alongside LNG because it is a cheap, domestic energy source in many affected consuming countries, and governments are increasingly framing all domestic energy sources, including coal, under a national-security umbrella.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Coal names in consuming countries could see policy tailwinds previously reserved for renewables, as 'national security' framing overrides prior climate-policy resistance to coal investment.

insights.claim: Countries are broadly adopting a 'Chinese model' of linking domestic energy sources to national security, which lets governments push through subsidies and policies for domestic energy (across natural gas, coal, and renewables) that previously stalled due to cost or climate-policy opposition.
insights.speaker: guest, Dr. Anas Alhajji (Energy Outlook Advisors)
insights.so_what: Expect a broad wave of national-security-justified energy subsidies across multiple energy sources and countries, benefiting domestic energy producers regardless of political party in power.

insights.claim: The MacroVoices trading desk's trade of the week is long Cheniere Energy (LNG) shares near $255.83 on a technical breakout above its descending trendline from the March peak, paired with a $240-strike protective put (36 days to expiry, costing about 1.8% of the stock price) to guard against a failed breakout.
insights.speaker: Patrick (trading desk co-host)
insights.so_what: A concrete, hedged way to express the LNG structural-winner thesis discussed in the interview, with a defined technical stop level at $240.
insights.quote: buying the $240 strike put for $4.55, giving us 36 days of protection

insights.claim: Large speculator positioning in S&P 500 futures surged from a one-year score of 16 to 94 in a single month, indicating the rally is being driven by systematic, momentum-chasing money rather than conviction built up over time.
insights.speaker: trading desk co-host (COT Signal analyst)
insights.so_what: A rapid, one-month positioning surge to the top decile is fragile; a market rollover could trigger fast CTA-driven unwinding, so equity longs should watch for a break below roughly 7,400 on the S&P 500 as a systematic sell-trigger zone.
insights.quote: large speculators positioning in the S&P futures has surged from 16 to 94 and that just happened in a single month

insights.claim: Large speculator positioning in WTI crude has fallen back to a one-year score of just 19, near pre-war levels, meaning nearly all of the war-risk premium that speculators added during the Iran conflict has already been unwound even as the conflict re-escalates.
insights.speaker: trading desk co-host (COT Signal analyst)
insights.so_what: Futures positioning has essentially 'unpriced' the ongoing conflict, creating room for speculative long-building (and a price move higher) if renewed Bab el-Mandeb or Hormuz escalation is confirmed.
insights.quote: the market spent the last month unpricing a war that never actually ended

insights.claim: Coffee futures saw large speculator positioning jump from a one-year score of 0 to 51 in a single month, the largest such increase across all tracked assets, after an eight-month, 44% bear market bottomed on Brazilian weather damage, El Nino concerns, and thin global inventories, yet positioning remains far below the historical ~40% net-long peak.
insights.speaker: trading desk co-host (COT Signal analyst)
insights.so_what: Coffee's repricing looks driven by short-covering rather than crowded new-long positioning, leaving room for the rally to extend if price, open interest, and large-speculator buying continue to rise together.
insights.quote: Large specs are net long only about 15% of their total open interest compared to their historical peak, which is usually around 40%.
